summary: Japanese ukiyo-e artist of the Utagawa school. Most commonly identified by The Fifty-Three Stations of the Tokaido and One Hundred Famous Views of Edo. Last great ukiyo-e master. Van Gogh copied his prints. Also painted Thirty-Six Views of Mount Fuji and bird-and-flower prints.

works.name: The Fifty-Three Stations of the Tokaido (1833–1834)
works.description: Woodblock print series depicting locations along the Tokaido road from Edo to Kyoto. Begins with Nihonbashi Bridge. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Woodblock print series depicting stations along the Tokaido road from Edo to Kyoto; begins at Nihonbashi Bridge
examples: name this ukiyo-e master...who created the The Fifty-Three Stations of the Tokaido
details the protagonist's journey from Edo and Kyoto on the Tokaido road

clues.clue: Servant covers his master, men struggle uphill in rain; man with yellow umbrella blown downhill (Shono station)
examples: A servant stands bare-backed, having taken off his yellow jacket to cover his master

works.name: One Hundred Famous Views of Edo (1856–1858)
works.description: Series of woodblock prints depicting scenes from Edo. Includes Sudden Shower over Shin-Ohashi Bridge and Plum Park in Kameido. Van Gogh copied several of these. Finished after Hiroshige's death by his student.

clues.clue: Sudden Shower: thin slanted black lines represent rain over a bridge; people run for cover; Van Gogh copied as Bridge in the Rain
examples: uses many thin, slanted, black lines to represent a sudden rain over a bridge
people on a wooden bridge run for cover during a sudden shower

clues.clue: Plum Park in Kameido: portrait-oriented, abstracted branches of 'Sleeping Dragon' tree; Van Gogh copied; prominent horizontal branch and red hue inspired Gauguin's Vision After the Sermon
examples: features a prominent horizontal branch and red hue, which inspired elements in Paul Gauguin's Vision After the Sermon and was copied by Vincent Van Gogh
shows the abstracted branches of a tree nicknamed "Sleeping Dragon"

recursive_suggestions: Ukiyo-e — broader topic; cross-topic with Hokusai, Kuniyoshi, Sharaku
Japonism / Van Gogh and Japan — cross-topic
comprehensive_summary:
Utagawa Hiroshige was a Japanese ukiyo-e artist of the Utagawa school, considered the last great master of ukiyo-e. His most famous series is The Fifty-Three Stations of the Tokaido (1833–1834), depicting locations along the road from Edo to Kyoto, beginning at Nihonbashi Bridge.

His One Hundred Famous Views of Edo includes the iconic Sudden Shower over Shin-Ohashi Bridge (thin slanted black lines for rain; people running for cover) and Plum Park in Kameido (portrait-oriented, abstracted branches of the 'Sleeping Dragon' tree). Vincent van Gogh copied several of these prints, including Bridge in the Rain and Plum Park. The plum tree print's prominent horizontal branch and red hue also inspired Gauguin's Vision After the Sermon. The series was finished after his death by his student, who adopted his name.

Hiroshige also created Thirty-Six Views of Mount Fuji (like Hokusai) and hundreds of bird-and-flower prints. He was parodied by Kuniyoshi using cats with punny names. An eagle dominates a print of a snow-covered plain.
